1: wayyisaʿ — waw-consecutive Qal imperfect of nāsaʿ (to pull up tent-pegs, break camp, set out). The term is the technical vocabulary of nomadic movement: pulling the tent-pegs signals the departure of the entire camp. ʾĕlōhê ʾāḇîw yiṣḥāq — "the God of his father Isaac": the patriarchal covenant-designation locates YHWH within the father-to-son covenantal chain rather than as an abstract deity. The relational genealogy of the divine name is the covenant-formula.
2: bĕmarʾōt hallaylāh — "in night visions" (marʾôt, plural of marʾeh, vision/appearance); the plural is an intensive form indicating the vividness or multiplicity of the vision experience. Yaʿăqōḇ yaʿăqōḇ — the double-name call (also 22:11 Abraham; Exodus 3:4 Moses; 1 Samuel 3:10 Samuel) is the theophanic urgency formula. hinnēnî — Jacob's response of total covenantal availability; the same word used by Abraham at the Akedah (Genesis 22:1,7,11).
3: ʾānōkî hāʾēl — "I am the God" with the definite article (hāʾēl). The article on ʾēl is unusual; it specifies the particular divine identity against any other use of the title ʾēl in the surrounding Canaanite/Egyptian context. ʾal-tîrāʾ — the reassurance-formula of covenant-initiation encounters (also 26:24 to Isaac; Genesis 46:3; Isaiah 41:10); its appearance marks this as a structurally parallel theophany to the covenant-renewals of Abraham (Genesis 15:1) and Isaac (Genesis 26:24).
4: ʾānōkî ʾērēḏ ʿimmĕkā — "I myself will go down with you" (ānōkî is emphatic: "I, personally"). The divine descent-promise uses the same yāraḏ (go down) vocabulary as YHWH's judicial descents (Genesis 11:5; Genesis 18:21); here it is a presence-promise, not a judgment-descent. ʿālōh ʾaʿaʿlĕkā — infinitive absolute + finite verb: "I will surely bring you up" — the double-verb construction is the Hebrew idiom for absolute certainty (cf. mōt tāmût, "you will surely die," Genesis 2:17). The word "bring up" (ʿālāh) anticipates the Exodus (ʿālāh from Egypt).
5: hāʿăgālōt — "the carts/wagons" (ʿăgālāh, wheeled vehicle). The term is significant in later OT narrative: the Philistines will use an ʿăgālāh to return the Ark (1 Samuel 6:7-12), and David's initial ark-transport used an ʿăgālāh with fatal consequences (2 Samuel 6:3). The Egyptian-supplied carts as the vehicle for Jacob's Egypt-descent is a narrative marker of Pharaoh's genuine provision for the covenant-family at this point in Joseph's story.
6: ûḇēn-bĕnāyw — "and his grandsons with him" alongside "his daughters and granddaughters" (bĕnōtāyw ûḇĕnôt bānāyw). The verse's Hebrew lists four generations: Jacob himself, sons, grandsons, daughters/granddaughters — the comprehensive household unit. The repetition of "all his descendants" (kol-zarʿô, all his seed) across vv6-7 emphasizes the totality of the covenant-family's migration: no branch of the Abrahamic promise stays behind.
7: bānāyw ûḇnê-bānāyw ʾittô — sons and grandsons; bĕnōtāyw ûḇĕnôt bānāyw — daughters and granddaughters. The genealogical formula is structured by gender pairs: male line first (sons, grandsons), then female line (daughters, granddaughters). The inclusivity of the formula — explicitly naming both male and female descendants — is notable; the numerical count that follows (vv8-27) will count males in the genealogical list, but this verse establishes that "all his descendants" includes both sexes.
8: ʾēlleh šĕmôt bĕnê yiśrāʾēl — "these are the names of the sons of Israel" — the genealogical header formula that will recur verbatim at the beginning of Exodus (Exodus 1:1: wĕʾēlleh šĕmôt bĕnê yiśrāʾēl). The echo is deliberate: the genealogy of those who "went to Egypt" in Genesis 46 is the genealogy of those who "were in Egypt" at the start of Exodus. The name-list of the seventy is the literary bridge between the patriarchal and Mosaic eras.
9: The sons of Reuben: Ḥănôk wĕpallûʾ wĕḥeṣrôn wĕḵarmî. These four names reappear in the wilderness census (Numbers 26:5-7) confirming the genealogical record's consistency across the Pentateuch. The brevity of the listing — just names, no comments — follows the standard ANE genealogical register format: the name is the data; additional biographical information is not the genre's purpose.
10: ûḇen-hayyĕmînî — "and Benjamin." Wait — this verse is Simeon's sons. wĕšāʾûl ben-hakkĕnaʿănît — "Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman." The specification of Shaul's Canaanite mother is the only ethnic marker in this genealogy for a mother — it may reflect the mixed-heritage situation of the Simeonite line and is possibly related to the Shechem episode of Genesis 34, where Simeon played a leading role in the violence against a Canaanite city. The ethnic note distinguishes this son within the Simeonite family.
11: The sons of Levi: Gēršôn qĕhāt ûmĕrārî — the three Levitical families that will structure the entire wilderness Tabernacle service and later Temple service. The Kohathites carry the ark and most sacred furniture (Numbers 3:31; Genesis 4:4-20); the Gershonites carry the tent-coverings (Numbers 3:25-26; Genesis 4:21-28); the Merarites carry the frames (Numbers 3:36-37; Genesis 4:29-33). The three names here are simultaneously genealogical data and the structural foundation of Israel's future worship system.
12: wayĕhî ʿēr wĕʾônān bĕʾereṣ kĕnaʿan — "Er and Onan had died in the land of Canaan." The parenthetical note (also signaled by the waw-disjunctive + imperfect construction) refers back to Genesis 38; the deaths of Er and Onan are included in the genealogy's count even though they did not go to Egypt. Ḥeṣrôn wĕḥāmûl — Perez's sons are included even though they were born very close to this time (or possibly after); the genealogy's counting may include anticipated descendants as well as those alive at the moment of departure.
13: The sons of Issachar — tôlāʿ ûpûwwāh wĕyôḇ wĕšimrôn. The name Puah (Pûwāh) appears later as one of the Hebrew midwives in Egypt (Exodus 1:15: Pûʿāh) — a possible connection, though the different spellings may indicate different persons. The Issachar genealogy is otherwise unremarkable in its literary form; its function is numerical (contributing to the 70 count) and genealogical (establishing the tribal lineage).
14: The sons of Zebulun — sereḏ wĕʾēlôn wĕyaḥlĕʾēl. These three names do not appear prominently in later biblical narrative, consistent with Zebulun's relatively minor role in the tribal history compared to Judah, Ephraim, or Levi. The genealogy's consistent three-to-seven sons per tribe reflects historical memory of the tribal sub-clan structure rather than invented roundness.
15: šĕlōšîm wĕšālōš — "thirty-three in all" for Leah's line. The counting formula for each matriarchal group — "these were [the mother's] children... [number] in all" — structures the genealogy in four maternal blocs: Leah (v15), Zilpah (v18), Rachel (v22), Bilhah (v25). The four-mother structure mirrors the narrative of the preceding chapters and makes explicit the covenant-family's organizational principle: the four women, their sons, and their descendants.
16: The sons of Gad — seven names (ṣipyôn wĕḥaggî šûnî wĕʾeṣbôn ʿĕrî wĕʾărōḏî wĕʾarʾēlî). Gad's seven sons give a relatively large sub-clan. The numbers vary across the genealogical lists (Gad's sons in Numbers 26:15-18 have six names with different spellings), reflecting the usual ANE genealogical variation across independent genealogical registers. The variant spellings are not errors but different transliterations or tribal nicknames.
17: wûḇĕnôtêhem śerāḥ — "their sister was Serah." The naming of a daughter is exceptional in this male-dominated genealogy; Serah appears again in Numbers 26:46 (also exceptional: a named daughter in a census) and in the tradition is associated with extraordinary longevity (some rabbinic traditions make her the one who informed Jacob that Joseph was alive, Genesis 45:26). The listing of a named daughter indicates she was remembered as genealogically significant — perhaps as the originator of a recognizable Asherite sub-clan through her descendants.
18: šiššāh ʿāśār — "sixteen in all" for Zilpah's line. The closing parenthetical "(these were the children born to Jacob by Zilpah, whom Laban had given to his daughter Leah)" establishes the maternal identity for the counting section. The passive participial yĕlāḏāh (who was born) applies to Zilpah: "whom Laban had given" (ʾăšer nātan lāḇān lĕlēʾāh bittô). The reminder of Laban's gift of Zilpah frames Zilpah's children within the complicated Laban-household narrative of Genesis 29-31.
19: ûḇĕnê rāḥēl ʾēšet yaʿăqōḇ — "the sons of Jacob's wife Rachel" — Rachel is specifically designated "Jacob's wife" (ʾēšet yaʿăqōḇ), whereas Leah is simply listed without this honorific earlier (v15). The distinction may reflect the narrator's acknowledgment of Rachel's primacy in Jacob's affection (Genesis 29:30: "Jacob loved Rachel more than Leah"), or it may be the genealogy's convention for distinguishing the primary wife from the secondary concubines (Zilpah, Bilhah).
20: ʾăšer yāllĕḏāh-lô ʾāsĕnat bat-pôṭîperaʿ kōhēn ʾôn — the full parenthetical identification of Asenath: "daughter of Potiphera, priest of On." The specification of her priestly Egyptian lineage — On (= Heliopolis, the great sun-temple city of Egypt) — locates Ephraim and Manasseh as the grandsons of an Egyptian priest. This mixed heritage does not prevent their full adoption into Israel's tribal structure (Genesis 48:5); their covenant status is determined by Jacob's blessing-adoption, not by maternal ethnicity.
21: The sons of Benjamin — ten names. Benjamin's ten sons are remarkable in number (no other patriarch has ten sons listed here); the ten are the sub-clans of Benjamin. The number varies in different biblical genealogies (1 Chronicles 8:1-5 gives five sons for Benjamin; Numbers 26:38-41 gives five differently named sons), reflecting independent genealogical traditions for the Benjaminite tribal history. The ten-son count here gives Benjamin the largest immediate family, which is consistent with the extra attention Jacob pays to Benjamin (Genesis 42-45).
22: ʾarbaʿāh ʿāśār — "fourteen in all" for Rachel's line. The fourteen includes Joseph himself, Benjamin, and their sons (Joseph's two sons Ephraim and Manasseh are counted in v20, and Benjamin's ten sons are counted in v21 — together 14 from Rachel's line). The Rachel-group count is computed differently from the Leah-group: Rachel's sons and their sons are all direct descendants counted together.
23: ûḇen-dān ḥûšîm — "the son of Dan: Hushim." Dan has only one son listed here, consistent with the historical smallness of the Danite clan (Numbers 26:42-43 lists one Danite clan, Shuham). The singular son is linguistically significant: the only case in this genealogy of a singular son for a patriarch; the use of ben (singular) versus bĕnê (plural sons) in the other listings marks Dan's demographic situation. The Danites will later become one of the larger tribes by the wilderness census (Numbers 1:39: 62,700 — second only to Judah).
24: The sons of Naphtali — yaḥṣĕʾēl wĕgûnî wĕyēṣer wĕšillēm — four sons. Naphtali's four sub-clans appear in Numbers 26:48-50 with parallel names (Jahzeel/Jahziel, Guni, Jezer, Shillem/Shallum). The consistency across the census lists (Num 26) and this patriarchal register indicates a shared genealogical tradition, however independently transmitted. The Naphtali names include several theophoric elements: ʾēl in Jahzeel, yāh in Jezer (possibly).
25: šiḇʿāh — "seven in all" for Bilhah's line (Dan's one + Naphtali's four + their sons = seven; or the seven listed in vv23-25). The closing parenthetical for Bilhah mirrors the Zilpah-parenthetical of v18: the secondary wives' children are bracketed explicitly as "born to Jacob by [wife], whom Laban had given to [his daughter]." The repetition of Laban's role in providing both secondary wives frames their children within the larger story of Jacob's complex family-formation in Paddan-Aram.
26: yōṣĕʾê yĕrēḵô — "those who came out from his loins/thigh" — the idiom for biological descent. Yārēk (thigh/loin) is the euphemism for the procreative organ; the same body-part appears in the oath-gesture (Genesis 24:2,9; Genesis 47:29: "put your hand under my thigh"). The count of 66 (šiššîm wĕšēš) excludes Joseph (already in Egypt) and his two sons — only direct descendants who made the journey. The distinction between 66 (those who went) and 70 (the full family count) is clarified in the next verse.
27: šiḇʿîm — "seventy in all." The MT count of 70 (66 + Joseph + Ephraim + Manasseh = 69; Jacob himself makes 70 — or the two sons of Joseph + Joseph = 3 added to 66 + 1 = 70 with Jacob). The LXX counts 75, following a different calculation that adds five more descendants of Joseph (cited in Acts 7:14; Stephen uses the LXX). Deuteronomy 10:22 gives 70 (agreeing with the MT here). The discrepancy is a known manuscript/counting-tradition difference; the number 70 is symbolically complete (70 nations of the earth in Gen 10; 70 elders of Israel in Exodus 24:1; Numbers 11:16).
28: lĕhôrōt lĕpānāyw — "to direct/show the way" before him. The verb yārāh (Hiphil: to direct, teach, point the way — also the root of tôrāh, instruction/law) is used for Judah's advance-scouting mission. Judah is sent ahead "to teach/direct" the way to Goshen — the same Judah who was the spokesman for the brothers before Joseph (Genesis 44:14-34) and who offered himself as a surety for Benjamin (Genesis 44:33). Judah's leadership role in these final chapters anticipates the Judah-tribe's covenant-leadership role in later Israel.
29: wayyipōl ʿal-ṣawwĕʾrāyw — "he fell upon his neck" — the most intimate physical embrace in the OT idiom: falling on the neck (throwing one's arms around the other's neck) is the full-bodied embrace of intense emotional reunion (also Genesis 45:14: Joseph embracing Benjamin; Genesis 45:15: embracing his brothers). wayyēḇk ʿōd — "and wept still/again" (ʿôd, yet/still/again) — the adverb indicates that Joseph wept at length, possibly in addition to previous weeping (Genesis 45:2,14-15). The reunion weeping is the most sustained of Joseph's repeated weepings.
30: ʾāmûtāh happaʿam — "let me die this time" — the cohortative form of māwet (to die), expressing resigned readiness or willingness: "I can die now / let me now die." The cohortative is the mode of volition — Jacob is not expressing despair but completion: having seen Joseph, his life's deepest longing is fulfilled. Simeon uses the same formula in reverse at Genesis 43:14 (kǎʾăšer šāḵaltî šākāltî); Jacob uses it here as a statement of contentedness with whatever comes next.
31: ʾeʿĕleh wĕʾaggîḏāh lĕparʿōh — "I will go up and tell Pharaoh" — Joseph's announcement uses ʿālāh (go up) for the journey to Pharaoh's court, consistent with the Egyptian social geography where the court was "above" in terms of social status (even if the Nile flows north/downward). The phrase "who were living in Canaan" (hayyōšĕḇîm bĕʾereṣ kĕnaʿan) frames the family's prior location for Pharaoh's information; the past tense ("were living") signals the permanent character of the move.
32: ʾanšê miqneh hāyû — "the men are men of livestock" (literally "men of cattle/stock they have been"). The Qal perfect hāyû expresses their established identity: this is what they have always been. ûḇĕʿîrām ûḇĕqārām ûḵol-ʾăšer lāhem hēḇîʾû — "and their flocks and herds and everything they own they have brought" — the inventory of what the family has brought signals both their pastoral identity and their economic status as a self-sufficient pastoral household, not refugees seeking charity.
33: mah-miʿăśêkem — "what is your occupation?" — the expected Pharaonic interrogation. Joseph's coaching (vv33-34) prepares the brothers for the formal court protocol: they should anticipate this question and answer it correctly. The conditional "when Pharaoh calls you in" (wĕhāyāh kî-yiqrāʾ lākem parʿōh) is the waw-consecutive + verb of condition, structuring the anticipated event as a foreseeable future that can be prepared for.
34: tôʿăḇat miṣrayim — "detestable to the Egyptians" — tôʿēḇāh is the strongest term for ritual/moral abomination in Hebrew; the same word used for idolatry (Deuteronomy 7:25-26), sexual violations (Leviticus 18:22-30), and the practices that made the Canaanites lose their land (Leviticus 18:27-28). Its application to shepherds in the Egyptian context may reflect the cultural memory of the Hyksos (the "shepherd kings" who ruled Egypt 1650-1550 BCE and left a traumatic xenophobic legacy), though this identification is debated. The motive is clear: the Egyptians' aversion to shepherds will ensure the Israelites are settled in Goshen (a frontier region) rather than integrated into Egyptian society — a providential separation.
